metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = lower-energy bonding state / ψ+ peaked on ions / conductor; CRIMSON = higher-energy antibonding state / ψ- peaked between ions / insulator gap
metadata.note: No Kronig-Penney determinant, no Bloch-theorem proof, no reciprocal-lattice formalism.

beats.narration_text: A metal, a semiconductor, and an insulator. They differ in one thing: a forbidden band of energies between the filled states and the empty ones. Where does that gap come from?
It opens because at one special wavelength, the lattice forces an electron to stand still — and it can do that two different ways. One way sits lower in energy. The gap is the distance between them.
What wavelength is special — and why does standing still come in two flavors with a forbidden zone between them?

props.headline: What wavelength is special — and why does standing still come…

card.headline:
Two standing waves, same kinetic energy.
Different positions → different potential energy → gap.
card.dek: The band gap: why a periodic lattice splits electron energies

beats.narration_text: Free electrons move as plane waves. Their energy climbs as a parabola: E equals h-bar-squared k-squared over 2m. No forbidden zones — every energy is allowed.
Now add a crystal. The lattice repeats every a. Bragg reflection couples waves at k equals pi over a to the wave at minus pi over a — they share the same wavelength, 2a.
At the zone boundary, the forward and backward waves are equal. They mix into two standing waves. The plus combination peaks on the ion sites. The minus combination peaks between them.

Both standing waves have the same kinetic energy. But the plus wave sits on the attractive ion cores — it sees lower potential energy. The minus wave avoids the cores — it sees higher potential energy. That difference is the gap.

The gap size equals twice the Fourier component of the lattice potential at the reciprocal lattice vector. Stronger ions mean a wider gap. The gap is the lattice writing itself into the electron spectrum.
Fill electrons up to the gap and you get an insulator. Fill halfway and the Fermi level cuts through a band — a metal. A small gap of 1 electron-volt makes a semiconductor.
Illustrative: silicon lattice constant 5.4 angstroms, band gap 1.1 eV. The Fourier component at the zone boundary is about 0.55 eV — half the gap, as the theory predicts.
At the Bragg wavelength the electron must stand still — two ways. One sits on the ions. One avoids them. Their energy difference is the forbidden gap. That is where band gaps come from.

props.headline: At the Bragg wavelength the electron must stand still — two…

card.headline:
Gap = 2|V_G|.
ψ₊ on ions (low E), ψ₋ between ions (high E).
card.dek: Bragg reflection at k = π/a forces two standing waves. Their potential energy difference is the band gap.
